required.title: A large sketchbook (at least 9x12 inches) and drawing materials

suggested.title: [Look Together] Artworks with strong visual texture

competence_markers.description: The child can describe an object in specific visual detail — going well beyond the label to notice color, texture, shape, light, and distinguishing features
The child draws regularly and freely, with confidence and without anxiety about the result
The child can complete an observation drawing of a simple object, spending at least 10 minutes looking and drawing
The child has built a texture collection of at least 10 different surfaces through rubbings, prints, or observational drawings
The child can sustain visual attention on a single artwork or object for at least one minute and narrate what they observed
The child demonstrates the habit of noticing — spontaneously commenting on visual details in daily life
The child distinguishes between drawing from observation (looking at the thing) and drawing from memory or imagination, and practices both
The child engages with art in person (museum, gallery) or in high-quality reproduction with genuine interest

narration_prompts.prompt: Pick up something nearby and tell me everything you can see about it.
narration_prompts.detail: Not what it is — what it actually looks like. Color, shape, texture, marks, wear, weight, light, shadow.

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a drawing you made this week.
narration_prompts.detail: What were you drawing? What did you notice while you were drawing it? What was hard? What surprised you?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between looking and recognizing?
narration_prompts.detail: Can you give me an example of each from today?

narration_prompts.prompt: Tell me about a texture you collected.
narration_prompts.detail: Where did you find it? What did it feel like? What did the rubbing look like?

narration_prompts.prompt: Describe a piece of art you looked at recently
narration_prompts.detail: — a painting, a drawing, a sculpture. What did you notice about it? What do you think the artist was looking at when they made it?

cross_connections.description: Both rhythm work and observation work train sustained attention and the perception of pattern. The child learning to keep a steady beat and the child learning to see the pattern in a leaf's veins are building the same attentional faculty through different senses.

The looking skills built here directly feed the color and making work in 3.4.5-A. The child who has learned to notice that shadows are not just "darker" but actually different in color is ready for the color mixing explorations in 3.4.5.

Observational drawing is the original scientific method. The child who draws a leaf from observation has studied that leaf more carefully than a child who merely looked at it. Darwin, Audubon, and da Vinci all drew what they studied.

Verbal description of visual experience and written narration develop the same precision of language. The child who can describe what they see can write about it; the child who draws what they see can describe it.
Sustained looking requires patience and the willingness to stay with a task. The slow look exercises develop the same executive function skills that IC2.1 builds through habit and routine.

content.why_this_matters:
You are going to learn how to really look at things. Not just glance and move on — really look. When most people see a tree, they think "tree" and stop seeing. But a tree is never just "a tree." It has a trunk that might be rough or smooth, dark or light, cracked or peeling. Its branches reach in different directions — some thick, some thin, some broken. Its leaves are not all the same shade of green. Some are in sunlight and some are in shadow. Some are new and bright, and some are old and dull. When you learn to see all of that, the world becomes a much more interesting place.

Drawing is one of the best ways to learn to see. When you draw something, you have to look at it carefully — really carefully — because your pencil can only put down what your eyes actually notice. Drawing is not about making pretty pictures (though sometimes that happens). Drawing is about training your eyes to see what is actually there instead of what your brain assumes is there.

You already know how to draw. Every child does. You have been making marks on paper since you could hold a crayon. This packet is not going to teach you to draw "correctly" — it is going to help you look more carefully, notice more, and keep drawing with the same freedom and confidence you have right now. That confidence is valuable. We are going to protect it while we build your seeing.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **The habit of careful looking** — the ability to observe an object, scene, or image with attention and detail, not just recognition
2. **Verbal description of visual experience** — the child can describe what they see in specific terms, not just labels
3. **Comfort and confidence with drawing** — the child draws regularly, freely, and without anxiety about the result
4. **Awareness of texture and pattern** — the child notices and records surface qualities in the world around them
5. **The beginning of observation drawing** — the child draws from what they see, not just from memory or imagination
6. **Joy in visual creation** — the child experiences drawing and mark-making as pleasurable, meaningful activity
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Learning to Look

Before you draw anything, you are going to practice looking. Looking is a skill — just like running or counting or reading. Most people think they already know how to look, but they are usually recognizing, not looking. Here is the difference:

**Recognizing** is when you see something and your brain says "that is a flower" and you move on. You know what it is. Done.

**Looking** is when you see the same flower and you notice: the petals are a pinkish-white on the outside and deeper pink on the inside. The stem has tiny hairs on it. One petal is slightly torn. The center of the flower is yellow, but not all one shade of yellow — it is darker in the middle. There is a small beetle sitting on one petal. The flower is leaning to the left because the stem is bent.

That is a very different experience from "that is a flower." And here is something wonderful: when you learn to really look at a flower, you start really looking at everything — faces, rooms, trees, clouds, the way light falls through a window in the morning.

### Part 2: Looking Exercises

These are exercises you will do regularly. They are not tests. They are practice for your eyes.

**The Detail Game:** Your mentor picks up an object — a shoe, a pinecone, an apple — and holds it up. You describe everything you can see about it. Not "it is a shoe" but all the specific things: the color, the laces (are they frayed? knotted? even?), the sole (thick? thin? scratched?), the shape (pointed? round? curved?). Your mentor can help by asking: "What else do you notice? What about the bottom? What about the stitching?"

**The Memory Look:** Look at a table with several objects on it for thirty seconds. Then close your eyes (or turn around) and describe everything you can remember seeing. Where was each object? What color was it? What was next to what? Then look again and see what you missed.

**The Close-Up Look:** Use a magnifying glass to look at something small: the surface of a leaf, the weave of fabric, the grain of wood, the skin of your hand. Describe what you see. What patterns do you notice? What textures? The world looks completely different at close range.

**The Slow Look:** Sit in one spot — in a room, in a garden, on a porch — and look at one thing for a full minute without talking or moving. Then describe what you saw. One minute feels like a long time. That is fine. The practice of sustained attention is the point.

### Part 3: Free Drawing

You also need time to draw whatever you want, however you want. This is not observation drawing — this is free drawing. Draw monsters, castles, your family, a battle, a garden, an imaginary animal, a map of a secret land. Draw big and use lots of color.

Free drawing matters because it keeps your confidence alive. It is where your imagination stretches. It is where you discover that you can put an idea from your head onto paper and it becomes real — other people can see it.

There are no rules in free drawing except these:
- Use the whole page, not just a tiny part in the corner
- Try different materials — crayon one day, marker the next, pencil the next
- Do not erase everything and start over because it does not look "right." Keep going.

### Part 4: Drawing from Observation

Now we combine looking and drawing. You are going to draw things that are right in front of you — not from your head, but from your eyes.

**Your Hand:** Hold out your hand and draw it. Really look at it while you draw. Your hand has wrinkles and bumps and fingernails and knuckles. It does not look like the simple hand shape your brain might want to draw. Draw what you see, not what you "know" a hand looks like.

**A Simple Object:** Put a shoe (or an apple, or a cup) on the table in front of you. Draw it. Look at it more than you look at your paper. Your drawing does not need to look perfect. It needs to show that you looked carefully.

**Something from Nature:** Draw a leaf, a flower, a stone, a shell. Nature is full of patterns and textures that reward careful looking. Scientists used to draw everything they studied because drawing forced them to notice details that just looking would miss.

### Part 5: Texture and Pattern

The world is full of textures — rough, smooth, bumpy, soft, scratchy, slippery. You can record textures by making **rubbings**: put thin paper over a textured surface (tree bark, a coin, a leaf with raised veins, a piece of burlap) and rub the side of a crayon across the paper. The texture appears like magic.

You can also record patterns by **printing**: dip the cut side of a potato, or the bottom of a leaf, or a piece of crumpled foil into paint and press it onto paper. Each object leaves its own mark — its own signature.

Start a **texture collection** in your sketchbook. On each page, record a different texture with a rubbing or a description and a drawing. Label where you found it. Over time, you will build a library of the textures in your world.

### Part 6: Art Is Everywhere

Go to a museum or gallery if you can. Stand in front of a painting that is bigger than you are. Notice that a painting on a wall is completely different from a painting on a screen — you can see the brushstrokes, the texture of the paint, the actual size of the thing. Try to look at one painting for a full minute.

If you cannot visit a museum, look at art in books — large, high-quality art books. Look at how different artists saw the world: Van Gogh saw it in swirling, thick strokes. Monet saw it in shimmering dots of color. Durer saw it in precise, tiny lines. Each of them was looking at the same world you are looking at. They just learned to see it in different ways.
content.formative_practices:
### Study 1: The Looking Journal [Ongoing]
Every day (or as close to every day as possible), the child describes one thing they saw in detail. This can be oral (narrated to the mentor) or drawn in the sketchbook with a verbal description. The goal is the habit of noticing. "Today I really looked at the bark on the big tree in the yard. It has deep cracks and the inside of the cracks is lighter than the outside."

### Study 2: Free Drawing Time [Ongoing — At Least 3 Times Per Week]
Unstructured drawing time with a variety of materials available. The child draws whatever they want. The mentor is nearby and interested but does not direct, correct, or suggest. If the child shows their work, respond with genuine interest and specific observation: "I notice you used a lot of blue in the sky — and you made the blue lighter near the horizon. Tell me about this part."

### Study 3: Observation Drawing Sessions [Weekly]
Once a week, set up a simple observation subject: a piece of fruit, a flower in a vase, a favorite toy, the child's own hand. The child draws from observation for 10-15 minutes. Remind them to look at the object more than they look at the paper. Praise looking, not results: "I saw you really studying the stem of that flower before you drew it."

### Study 4: Texture Collection [Ongoing Project]
The child builds a texture collection in their sketchbook through rubbings, prints, and observational drawings of surfaces. Aim for at least 10 different textures over the course of the packet. Take the rubbings kit on walks — bark, manhole covers, stone walls, brick paths all offer different textures.

### Study 5: The Slow Look [Monthly]
Visit a museum, gallery, or art book and practice the slow look: one artwork, one full minute of sustained attention. Then the child narrates what they saw. What did they notice first? What did they notice after looking longer? What do they wonder about? This exercise builds the habit of sustained visual attention that will serve them in every subsequent art packet.

### Study 6: Nature Drawing [Seasonal]
Each season, the child draws something from nature that shows the season: a bare branch in winter, a flower in spring, a sun-baked stone in summer, a colored leaf in fall. These four drawings, collected over the year, become a visual record of change and a demonstration of growing observation skill.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Verbal Description
**Developing well:** The child describes objects with multiple specific details — color, texture, shape, pattern, markings. Descriptions become more detailed over weeks. The child spontaneously notices and comments on visual details in daily life.

**Developing slowly:** Descriptions are single-label ("it is a leaf") with no further detail, even with prompting. The child does not seem to register visual specifics.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you modeling detailed observation yourself? Are the looking exercises happening regularly? Is the child being asked to describe in a pressured way (like a test) or in a natural, shared way (like a conversation)?

### 2. Drawing Confidence
**Developing well:** The child draws regularly and willingly. They show their work with pride. They try different subjects and materials. They do not express anxiety about whether drawings are "good."

**Developing slowly:** The child avoids drawing, says "I can't draw," erases compulsively, or only copies from other images rather than drawing from life or imagination.

**Diagnostic questions:** Has someone criticized the child's drawing — even subtly? Are they comparing themselves to older siblings or peers? Is the drawing time genuinely free, or does it feel like an assignment? Have you been correcting or "helping" the drawings?

### 3. Observation Drawing
**Developing well:** The child can sit with an object and draw it for 10-15 minutes, looking back and forth between object and paper. The drawing shows evidence of looking — specific shapes, details, proportions that reflect the actual object rather than a generic symbol.

**Developing slowly:** The child draws a quick symbol (the generic "house" shape, the lollipop "tree") and declares they are done within a minute or two. They do not look at the object while drawing.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the object interesting to the child? (A boring subject produces a bored response.) Are you sitting with them and looking too? Have you demonstrated contour-style looking — where you trace the edge of the object with your eye while your pencil moves?

### 4. Texture Awareness
**Developing well:** The child enjoys making rubbings and prints. They seek out new textures. They notice and comment on surface qualities: "This rock is smooth on one side and rough on the other."

**Developing slowly:** The child is not interested in textures and does not notice surface qualities.

**Diagnostic questions:** Have you done rubbings together? The activity itself is usually engaging — if the child has not connected with it, try different surfaces or materials.
mentor_guide.common_pitfalls:
### Pushing Realism Too Early
At ages 4-6, a child's drawing does not look like what they see, and that is completely normal. Their motor control, spatial reasoning, and hand-eye coordination are still developing. If you push for accuracy ("Make it look more like the real thing"), you will produce anxiety, not skill. The observation work in this packet gradually builds the perceptual foundation that will make representational drawing possible in A2 and Weathering Storms. Trust the process.

### Over-Structuring Drawing Time
Free drawing means free. Do not assign subjects, suggest improvements, or hover. Be available and interested, but let the child lead. If they draw the same thing every session (a common pattern — horses, dinosaurs, rockets), that is fine. Repetition builds fluency.

### Neglecting the Looking Exercises
The looking exercises might feel less "productive" than drawing, but they are the core of this packet. A child who develops the habit of careful observation will become a strong drawer naturally. A child who draws without observation training will eventually hit a wall.

### Comparing to Siblings or Peers
Every child's visual development follows its own timeline. Some children are intensely visual at 4; others do not engage deeply with observation until 6 or 7. Do not compare. The question is always: is this child looking more carefully than they were a month ago?
